My original intention with this was for testinfra to do the setup:
1. Start the Vagrant box
2. Prep the vagrant box with needed libraries so that our tests will run
3. Provide way to simplify creating the hooks that could be used during your CI testing and using Vagrant through testinfra where you focus on the outcome of tests rather than fussing around with getting vagrant to work. 
4. Testing shell scripts is hard, testing python is much easier and this library is tested and it should work with the latest versions of Vagrant and I totally plan on helping maintain it if you find this library helpful for testinfra.
5. This backend was primarily created to be used as a test fixture for (spin up, run your tests, tear it down) and repeat and only focusing on solid building blocks.

Because this is `external_script`. It does not really have a specific purpose and your use case is a bit different in that you only wanted a notification on last output... External scripts often have some intervals in them... and `format_notification` might confuse users in thinking that they'll be getting a notification on every interval... (say I put in 7200, then I expect a notification every two hours). This is not the case... so I'm not sure if we should even add `format_notification` here too... It's too early to tell.

What you wanted is minimally implemented.  Minimal is the keyword here.  If users come by wanting notification on every two hours, then yes, it would be easier to implement that minimally too... 

If we add buttons, then it's hard to undo them so I'm trying to avoid not adding unnecessary configs because if an user stop by with a PR containing something really useful for `external_script` and it requires a button, then by all means, we should let him do that.

`external_script` is hard in that it does not really have specific purposes... It might be better to enhance https://py3status.readthedocs.io/en/latest/configuration.html#special-on-click-commands to accept `{placeholders}` too, not just `$OUTPUT`...

And obviously, to enhance `on_click` too with new options to better address #1264.

Creating a copy of the env variables and updating the `job_env` won't actually update the environment itself:

```python
>>> import os
>>> job_env = os.environ.copy()
>>> job_env["HADOOP_USER_NAME"] = "bar"
>>> os.environ.get("HADOOP_USER_NAME", "not set")
'not set'
```

You'll need to update it directly:

```python
>>> os.environ["HADOOP_USER_NAME"] = "bar"
>>> os.environ.get("HADOOP_USER_NAME", "not set")
'bar'
```

@jamesls We talked about this where I think this should be consistent with the current interface of executors in python:
```py
In [1]: from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor

In [2]: def raise_exception():
   ...:     raise Exception()
   ...: 

In [3]: with ThreadPoolExecutor(1) as executor:
   ...:     future = executor.submit(raise_exception)
   ...:     

In [4]: future.result()
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
Exception                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-4-a3ab6647db03> in <module>()
----> 1 future.result()
```
I do agree though that we should have some interface for enabling fast exits of the transfer manager if any of the transfers fail such as:
```py
with TransferManager(client) as m:
   with m.raise_if_failures():
       m.upload('myfile', 'mynonexistentbucket', 'key') 
```

The way it's done for now is, all nonce-taking routes are marked as **not** retryable in wrapper_table.json, and then this line makes it so that they are actually eligible for retry, in these bindings (so, exactly the situation you are asking about, I think). That means there are sort of three classes of routes: not retryable (`retryable=false`), retryable with a nonce (`retryable=false` in the wrapper table but it's marked as retryable in the bindings), and retryable (`retryable=true`).

If at some point we decide that all language bindings are required to implement the nonce support correctly, then we could remove this special logic here and just mark all nonce-taking routes as actually retryable.


If I write that, given that `exactly_one` is true by default, we will always have that `limit=1`. Also, the result will always be a single location result (and not a list, even if a limit is given). To change this (i.e. have a limit other than one **and** a list of results), we would have to explicitly set `exactly_one` to false in addition to passing a limit, which is a bit cumbersome in my opinion.

I initially wanted to raise an exception if both exactly_one and limit were set, but as said above such a case is not ideal. That's why I made it so that if limit is given, the value of exactly_one is changed and determined by `exactly_one = limit == 1`.

For consistency with all other PathActions, `target_full_path` should point to the single path that's actually being mutated by that action, and in this case that would be the `record.json` file.

All of the path actions that go with unlink/link have a pretty clean concept of

    target_full_path = target_prefix + target_short_path

Here, I guess it's unclear what the equivalent of `target_prefix` is.  Is it `target_pkgs_dir`, or `package_cache_entry.extracted_package_dir` (which is a full, absolute path also)?

Looking at the code, for now it makes more sense to me to use the second, so we can keep the consistency with the other path actions.  So the init signature should take only two parameters, where the first is just the equivalent of `join(target_pkgs_dir, target_extracted_dirname)`.  Then, in init, also make a attribute `self.target_short_path = 'info/record.json`.  And the code for the `target_full_path` property should be

    return join(self.target_extracted_package_dir, win_path_ok(self.target_short_path))

And we maintain consistency with the rest of the code in the module.  If there's a good reason to break consistency though, then I doubt I'll have an objection.

This is a little bit weird. In fact, it is weird enough that I am pretty sure it is not what you want. You have _both_ a submodule and an explicit `git clone`. And the way you have them written are not equivalent.

`git submodule` tags a repository as a submodule to the outer repository, and the sub-repository is grafted in _at a specific commit_. That is why if you look farther down in this "files changed" view, you will see this:

![screenshot from 2017-07-28 07 59 30](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/4346/28723163-bd1f5746-736a-11e7-8f1b-be228631817b.png)

Notice how it says "added at 75c3a5". The repository is grafted in _at a specific point_.
Your `git clone` command, however, (1) plows over the graft, and (2) will clone HEAD. This is going to give you a repository in an inconsistent state. In some ways this is fine; CircleCI is never going to commit the result so it will hit the bit bucket as soon as the VM spins down. On the other hand, this inconsistency is likely to cause a decent bit of heartburn when contrasting local testing with CI as the gulf between 75c3a5 and HEAD widen.

I can not tell from reading this PR _which_ behavior you actually want. If your goal is to always use googleapis pinned to a specific point, then a submodule _might_ be a good answer. If it is (holding off on discussing that until I understand your goals), at minimum your `git clone` above needs to be replaced with `git submodule update --force`.

If your goal is to use googleapis at HEAD, submodules are very much the wrong tool. You would be far better off just `git clone`-ing the repo to a designated location. As I will say below, this will cause problems for local testing, though, which need to be talked through.

The `scale` method might be called by the user in the IPython interaction thread which is often different from the thread where the IOLoop is running.  As a result we want to avoid calling any coroutine like `retire_workers` directly.  Instead, we need to use the threadsafe method `IOLoop.add_callback`.  I suggest the following:

```python
@gen.coroutine
def f(to_close):
    yield self.scheduler.retire_workers(workers=to_close, ...)
    self.scale_down(to_close)

self.scheduler.loop.add_callback(f, to_close)
```

However this might become problematic if `scale_down` blocks (does it?  I'm not that knowledgable about the kubernetes API).   If so then we might want to offload this to a thread in a thread pool.

```python
from distributed.comm.utils import offload

@gen.coroutine
def f(to_close):
    yield self.scheduler.retire_workers(workers=to_close, ...)
    yield offload(self.scale_down, to_close)

self.scheduler.loop.add_callback(f, to_close)
```

This is one of the things I should have discussed in more detail in the original post. The interface change here is prompted by the need to support the new multi-phase initialization (PEP 489). I haven't actually implemented it yet (it's on the TODO list for this PR) but it requires this interface change. PEP 489 splits up the creation and initialization of modules into separate phases. This makes it possible to support subinterpreters and interpreter reloading. To enable this, it's expected that users only handle module initialization, but not creation.

I think multi-phase initialization support is important enough to warrant the new interface -- at least for embedded modules (although it's certainly possible to have the same interface with extension modules). Note that PEP 489 is only available for Python >= 3.5, but it should be possible backport some of the benefits to older versions of Python by doing a similar creation/initialization split within the macro.

The name change to `MODULE` was mostly done because of the interface change, to avoid having similarly named macros with completely different interfaces. Likewise, the `ADD` in the macro name is intentionally there to suggest that multiple embedded modules can be added to an executable -- as opposed to the single extension module.

The interface inconsistency with `PYBIND11_PLUGIN` is pretty bad and I would normally err on the side of consistency, but I feel that benefits of the new initialization scheme make it worth adopting, not just because of the embedded modules, but also as a way of future-proofing if the old module initialization scheme becomes deprecated within Python. A possible way to restore consistency would be to introduce a similar macro for extension modules:

```C++
PYBIND11_MODULE(name)(py::module &m) { ... } // extension -- only one possible
PYBIND11_ADD_EMBEDDED_MODULE(name)(py::module &m) { ... } // embedded -- any number
```

Certainly, that can be done, but my initial inquiry into the matter has led me to the conclusion that the result will actually be messier, mainly due to the way the unit tests will have to be written.

I think the resulting ugliness stems from the fact that `makeWellFormed` has no business choosing how to serialize a quoted string; using either single quotes or double quotes makes no difference to whether the result will be well formed—the choice should be the purview of a higher-level of abstraction, namely `serializeXML`, which makes the choice based on the criterion that the serialization should be as short as possible.

Also, what if an even higher level of abstraction (*e.g.*, the user) wants to ensure that every quoted string is delimited by double quotes, regardless of the length criterion? As it is now, it would be a simple matter of just telling `makeWellFormed` that a double quote is going to be used, and thereby letting `makeWellFormed` produced a well-formed value based on that fact.
